coredata.dc:title: Construction of polysulfone anion exchange hybrid membranes by incorporating carbon quantum dots and facilitated transport mechanisms
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Yuan
Zhang, Xiu
Feng, Tianci
Li, Meisheng
Xiao, Huifang
Zhou, Shouyong
Zhao, Yijiang
Zhong, Jing
Yang, Dawei
coredata.dc:description:
Low ion conductivity and poor alkali resistance are still the two major obstacles for the applications of anion exchange membranes (AEMs). In this work, the combined strategies of Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) hybrid and cross-linking modification via side-chains designing were employed to prepare organic–inorganic hybrid AEMs. Then, the cross-linked quaternized polysulfone (CQPSf-CQDs) hybrid AEMs by incorporating carbon quantum dots were successfully fabricated. After hybrid and cross-linking, the mechanical properties of CQPSf-CQDs hybrid AEMs were higher than that of the pristine one, reaching 49.4 MPa for the best one. Meanwhile, the existence of CQDs can expand the hydrophilic area in the membrane, which is beneficial to the transport of
O H
-
. The incorporated CQDs interact weakly with cationic groups in the side chains through hydrogen bonds. The simulation results further demonstrated that the introduction of CQDs was beneficial to the construction of hydrophilic and hydrophobic domains, thereby increasing
O H
-
conductivity. In addition, the prepared hybrid membranes own excellent dimensional stabilities and ion transport performances. These preliminary results give us a lot of hopes for further experiments and optimizations for the hybrid AEMs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anion exchange membranes
Hybrid
Carbon quantum dots
Cross-linking

Ion exchange capacity and ion conductivity testing
Preparation and characterizations of hybrid AEMs
Thermal and mechanical stabilities of hybrid AEMs
Water uptakes, swelling ratios, and ion exchange capacities of hybrid AEMs
Anion conductivity and alkaline stability of hybrid AEMs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication and characterization of CQDs. (a) The fabrication process and mechanism of CQDs. (b) and (c) The TEM morphology and particle size distribution of CQDs. (f) and (e) FTIR and XRD spectrum of CQDs. (d) Photographs of the H2O and CQDs aqueous under visible and UV lights, respectively.
XPS spectrum of CQDs: (a) full spectrum, (b) high resolution C1s spectrum, (c) high resolution N1s spectrum and (d) high resolution O1s spectrum.
1H NMR spectra of (a) PSf, (b) CMPSf and (c) CQPSf.

Fabrication and characterizations of the prepared hybrid AEMs. (a) The fabrication process and transport mechanisms for the CQPSf-CQDs hybrid membrane. (b) and (c) Photographs of the CQPSf and CQPSf-1% CQDs AEMs under visible and UV lights, respectively. (d) and (e) SEM morphologies of the CQPSf and CQPSf-1% CQDs AEM surfaces, respectively. (f) FTIR spectrum of different membranes. (g) and (h) SAXS pattern of CQPSf and CQPSf-1%-CQDs membranes.
TGA curves (a) and mechanical properties (b) of hybrid AEMs with different contents of CQDs.
Water uptakes (a), swelling ratios (b), surface water contacts (c) and ion exchange capacities (d) of hybrid AEMs with different contents of CQDs.
Anion conductivity (a) and alkaline stability (b) of hybrid AEMs.

(a)GQDs, (b)O-CQDs, (c)O-CQDs model diagram,(d) Diagram of the N-CQDs molecular model in water, (e) The interaction energy between GQDs, O-CQDs, NO-CQDs and water molecules and the number of intramolecular hydrogen bonds, (f) MSD for water molecules and hydroxides in CQDs, O-CQDs and NO-CQDs systems, (g) RDF analysis of oxygen atom (O(H2O)) in water and oxygen atom (O(–COOH) in carboxylic acid, nitrogen atom in amino group (N(–NH2)).
Performance of methanol/air (CO2-free) fuel cell at 60 °C for the CQPSf-1%CQD membrane.
Preparation process of CQPSf-CQDs hybrid AEMs.
Ionic conductivities (IC) and swelling ratios (SR)of various membranes in this work and the literatures.

head.ce:title: Construction of polysulfone anion exchange hybrid membranes by incorporating carbon quantum dots and facilitated transport mechanisms

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Regional Modern Agriculture & Environmental Protection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Low ion conductivity and poor alkali resistance are still the two major obstacles for the applications of anion exchange membranes (AEMs). In this work, the combined strategies of Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) hybrid and cross-linking modification via side-chains designing were employed to prepare organic–inorganic hybrid AEMs. Then, the cross-linked quaternized polysulfone (CQPSf-CQDs) hybrid AEMs by incorporating carbon quantum dots were successfully fabricated. After hybrid and cross-linking, the mechanical properties of CQPSf-CQDs hybrid AEMs were higher than that of the pristine one, reaching 49.4 MPa for the best one. Meanwhile, the existence of CQDs can expand the hydrophilic area in the membrane, which is beneficial to the transport of
O H
-
. The incorporated CQDs interact weakly with cationic groups in the side chains through hydrogen bonds. The simulation results further demonstrated that the introduction of CQDs was beneficial to the construction of hydrophilic and hydrophobic domains, thereby increasing
O H
-
conductivity. In addition, the prepared hybrid membranes own excellent dimensional stabilities and ion transport performances. These preliminary results give us a lot of hopes for further experiments and optimizations for the hybrid AEMs.

ce:section.ce:para: Under the “double carbon targets,” fuel cells have become one of the critical technologies for the “zero-carbon energy reconstruction.” Among different fuel cells, anion exchange membrane fuel cells (AEMFCs) have attracted extensive attentions due to their distinctive advantages, such as fast reaction kinetics, high electrode reaction efficiencies, and the use of non-noble metal catalysts, and running products only water [1]. In this fuel cell, the anion exchange membrane (AEM) is a key part, which can transport hydroxide ions and divide cathode and anode.

Currently, the main technical bottlenecks for applying AEMFCs are still the low conductivity and poor chemical stability of anion exchange membranes (AEMs) [2]. So far, various strategies have been used to overcome these shortcomings. Among them, side-chain modifications can improve the ion conductivity and alkali resistance stability of AEMs via designing the molecular structure and optimizing the microphase separation morphology in the membrane [3,4]. For examples, Chu et al. [5] synthesized two types of quaternized polyphenylene (PPO) with different lengths (n or m) and cationic side chains (PPO-C-nQA with triazole group in the side chain and PPO-m-QA without triazole group in the side chain) were prepared. Compared with PPO-m-QA without a triazole group on the side chain, the triazole group on the side chain of PPO-C-nQA could act as a weak Brnsted base. This Brnsted base provides more binding sites for water and hydroxide transport, resulting in higher water uptake (WU) and ionic conductivity (WU of PPO-C-nQA membranes (n = 1, 3, or 4) 55.7–79%, OH– conductivity 30–40.6 mS cm−1). These values are higher than PPO-m-QA membranes (m = 4, 6 and 7; water absorption 27.9–54.9 wt%, OH– conductivity 20.2–35.8 mS cm−1). In addition, this phenomenon occurs in other AEMs and PEMs with triazole groups [6–9]. Moreover, the length of side-chains played an important role in preventing the degradation of main chains under alkaline conditions. PPO-C-1QA with a methylene linker was degraded by SN2 substitution in a robust alkali environment, while PPO-C-nQA and PPO-C-mQA (n > 2 and m > 4) in the same environment was mainly due to PPO skeleton hydrolysis. These indicate that the long side-chains can alleviate the attacking of
O H
-
on the side-chain groups and enhance the alkali resistance of the membrane.

On the other hand, introducing suitable inorganic materials as fillers into AEMs can further improve the performance of the membrane, and the obtained hybrid AEMs not only owned good mechanical properties and chemical stabilities, but also have good electrochemical properties [10]. Generally, the surfaces and inner pores of inorganic nanomaterials own abundant active sites, thereby being conducive to further grafting different conductive groups on them, which would improve ion conductivities and mechanical properties of AEMs [11]. Rathod et al. [12] successfully introduced functionalized boron nitride (f-BN) into quaternized polyphenylene oxide (QPPO) to prepare nanohybrid AEMs. Then, the water contents and ion transport properties were enhanced obviously due to intermolecular hydrogen bonds between the QPPO matrix and f-BN. In our previous work, natural hydrophilic nanofiber-like palygorskite was introduced into the quaternary ammonium functionalized polysulfone to prepare high-performance AEMs. It is found that the hybrid of nanofiber-like palygorskite not only increased
O H
-
conductivity of AEMs, but also enhanced their mechanical and alkali resistance properties [13]. Besides, many more inorganic materials have been used to modify AEMs, e.g. SiO2 [14–16], ZrO2 [17–19], TiO2 [20–22], carbon nitride [23–25], carbon nanotubes [26–28] and gaphene [29–31]. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely used for nanosensors [32–34], catalyzers [35–40], biomedical field [41–45], batteries [46–48], etc., because of small size effects, biocompatibility, and excellent mechanical and optical properties. To now, CQDs have been employed to add into membranes to improve performances, such as proton exchange membranes [49,50], nanofiltration membranes [51–53], and ultrafiltration membranes [54], and so on. For AEMs, it has been reported that the CQDs are incorporated into polyethersulfone (PSf) AEMs to enhance the
O H
-
conductivity and physicochemical properties [55]. Compared with pure PSf AEMs, the hybrid AEM with only 1.0% quaternized carbon dots (QCDs) owned a twofold boosting in
O H
-
conductivity and a 13% increase in alkali resistance. It was confirmed that a small amount of QCDs doping could control the ion transport capacity, and improve membrane performances. However, only hybrid with CQDs for the type of short chains PSf AEMs still had a poor alkali resistance and faced degradation by
O H
-
attacking. Thus, how about combining these two strategies for AEMs?

Herein, the two strategies of cross-linking modification by side-chains designing and CQDs hybrid were combined to prepare cross-linked organic–inorganic hybrid AEMs. Quaternized polysulfone and carbon quantum dots (QPSf-CQDs) were selected as the membrane’s backbone and filler, respectively. Effects of side groups and amounts of CQDs were investigated systematically. To further understand ion transport mechanisms, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations were used to analyze CQDs behaviors in the water environment. In the end, the performances of a single battery were tested using an obtained hybrid AEM.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA, 99%), Ethylenediamine (EDA, 99.5%), Dichloromethane (DCM, 99.8%), Paraformaldehyde (POM, 95%), Chlorotrimethylsilane (99%), anhydrous tin tetrachloride (SnCl4, 99%), NNDimethylacetamide (DMAC, 99.8%), 2-[2-(Dimethylamino)ethoxy]ethanol (DMEE, 98%), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 95%), Benzyl alcohol (BnOH, 99.5%), N,N,N′,N′-Tetramethylethylenediamine (TEMED, 98%) were all purchased from Aladdin. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 98%) was offered by local suppliers. Polyethersulfone (PSf, Colorless crystal) was supplied by Wuxi Lianhengzhou Chemical Co.China. Deionized water was self-made in the lab. PSf was dried in a vacuum oven before use. Other reagents were used as received.

Chloromethylation of PSf was carried out referring to the procedure reported by Duan et al. [56]. Firstly, 3.0 g dried PSf was dissolved in 150 mL dichloromethane in a three-neck round bottom flask to obtain a homogenous solution. Then, 1.02 g paraformaldehyde was added and stirred for mixing for half an hour. Secondly, 0.086 mL SnCl4 and 8.3 mL chlorotrimethylsilane were added dropwise. After that, the mixed solution was stirred and refluxed at 40 °C for 36 h under the nitrogen protection. Thirdly, the answer was precipitated in ethanol and washed in ethanol 3 times. The chloromethylation of PSf was dried in an oven at 80 °C for 24 h to obtain samples (CMPSf).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Designing of side-chain and cross-linked PSf
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis process of side-chain and cross-linked quaternized PSf (CQPSf) was shown in Scheme 1 . The dried CMPSf was dissolved in DMAC and stirred at 35 °C for 30 min to form a uniform solution of 10 w/v%. Then, TEMED was added into CMPSf/DMAc solution and rushed at 45 °C for 24 h to prepare CQPSf, and the dried product was named CQPSf.

The fabrication process and mechanism of CQDs are provided in Fig. 1 (a). At first, 1.0507 g CA was dissolved in 10 mL ultra-pure water, then 0.335 mL EDA was added and mixed. Secondly, this mixture was transferred into a reactor and reacted at 150 °C for 5 h, followed by natural cooling to room temperature. Thirdly, the cooled solution was filtered by a polyethersulfone filter with a mean pore size of 0.22 μm, then purified by a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 3500 Da for 2 days, and freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain CQDs powder.

As shown in Scheme 1, a certain amount of CQDs power was dispersed in DMAc to prepare the homogeneous solution. This homogeneous solution was added to CQPSf and stirred under ultrasound for 1 hour. Then, the mixture was stirred at 45 °C for 4 h in an oil bath. After that, the resultant solution was cast on a glass plate using a drawknife for the desired thickness, followed by vacuum drying for 24 h at 80 °C. At last, the membrane was soaked in 1 mol L−1 NaOH solution at room temperature for 24 h, and then washed and soaked in deionized water for 24 h, respectively. The prepared membranes were designated as CQPSf-x-CQDs, where x was the mass amount of CQDs, which were 0%, 1%, 2.5% and 5%, respectively.

The structures, functional groups, and morphologies of synthesized CQDs and hybrid membranes were thoroughly characterized using various analytical techniques. The chemical structures for hybrid membranes were analyzed by 1H NMR (Bruker AVANCE II 400 spectrometer) using CDCl3 and DMSO solvents. The functional groups of CQDs and membranes were obtained from FT-IR spectra on a Nicolet FT-IR spectrometer. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out on a NETZSCH TGA-2 thermogravimetric analyzer under a nitrogen atmosphere with a heating rate of 10 ◦C/min. Small Angle X-ray Scattering (SAXS) was obtained using a Switzerland X-ray diffraction (XRD, ARL/X, TRA). The mechanical properties of AEMs were measured 3 times for each sample on a SHIMADZU AGS-X10KN automated testing instrument at an elongation rate of 1 mm/min. The testing size of AEMs was 1 cm × 7 cm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water uptake and swelling ratio testing
ce:section.ce:para:
Water uptake (WU) was calculated using the following Eq. (1).
(1)
W U
%
=
W w
-
W d
W d
× 100 %
where W d and W w are the weights of dry and wet membranes, respectively.

Dimensional stability was characterized by the swelling ratio (SR, %), represents the length or volume expansion after membrane hydration. The SR was calculated by Eq. (2).
(2)
S R
%
=
L w
-
L d
L d
× 100 %
where L w and L d are the lengths of dry and wet membranes, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ion exchange capacity and ion conductivity testing
ce:section.ce:para:
The ion exchange capacity (IEC) is determined via acid-base titration: Initially, the AEM samples were soaked into an HCl (0.01 mol L−1) solution for 24 h to convert all charge sites into the Cl− form. Using phenolphthalein as an indicator, 0.01 mol/L NaOH was used to reverse titrate the HCl solution, and the IEC was determined by utilizing the following relationship (3):
(3)
IEC =
(
V a
-
V b
)
×
C
NaOH
/
W d
where Va denotes the consumed volume of the blank sample, Vb denotes the consumed volume of the NaOH solution,
C
N a O H
is the concentration of the NaOH solution, and Wd is the weight of the dry membrane.

The in-plane ion conductivities (
σ
) of membranes were determined by the two-probe AC impedance spectroscopy method on a Chen Hua electrochemical workstation. The ion conductivity (σ, mS cm−1) was calculated using Equation:
(4)
σ = L / R A
where L, R and A are the length between two electrodes, the AC Impedance Value of the membrane, and the cross-sectional area of the membrane, respectively.

A direct methanol fuel cell device was self-made and tested at 60 °C. The membrane electrode was hot-pressed with platinum-supported carbon paper at 60 °C and 2 MPa, and the catalyst loading was 1 mg cm−2 (Pt/C). The fuel (2 mol L−1 NaOH and 2 mol L−1 methanol mixed solution) was passed into the anode at a rate of 2 ml·min−1, and the humidified air was passed into the cathode at a rate of 400 ml·min−1. During this period, the device was activated by heating at 60 °C. The relationship between the potential and the current on the membrane electrode was recorded by an electrochemical workstation (Chen Hua e60, China).

Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations were used to analyze CQDs behaviors in the water environment. Compass II was selected as the simulated system force field. The detailed setting and running methods were provided in our previous works [57]. In this work, three different CQDs models were constructed: The first one, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), had only methyl on the surface. The second one, oxygen-containing carbon quantum dots (O-CQDs), had hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface. The third one, nitrogen-containing carbon quantum dots (NO-CQDs), had hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups on the surface. Among three types of CQDs, the third CQDs (NO-CQDs) were prepared in this work. In CQDs models, the colors of atoms are as follows: C (gray), H (white), n (blue) and O (red)). To save the simulation time, the calculation size model of 1.2 nm was selected in this work.

The structure of the constructed CQDs was optimized in the Forcite module, and then the optimized CQDs were filled in a simulation box of 30 nm * 30 nm * 30 nm. After optimization, the solvent molecular water was randomly distributed in the simulation box using the Amorphous Cell module. Inside the blank space, the packing density was set as 1.0 g cm−3. The temperature and pressure were controlled by A Nosé−Hoover thermostat and Berendsen barostat algorithm, respectively. The cutoff distance for pair interactions was set to 12.5 Å. The contribution of calculating long-range interactions by the tail correction and the particle–particle–particle-mesh (PPPM) solver. For each system, the systems were equilibrated at 298 K in the NPT ensemble for 2 ns. Finally, a MD run in the NVT ensemble was performed for 3 ns for equilibrium, and a following 1 ns NVT simulation was used to obtain the data. The H-bond was determined when the distance between the hydrogen atom and the acceptor was less than or equal to 2.5 Å and the angle formed by the donor, hydrogen and acceptor atoms was larger than 139° [58].

Then, the interaction energy between the water molecules and CQDs was calculated according to the Equation 5 at 25 °C and 101 kPa.
E
inter
=
E
total
-
(
E
water
+
E
CQDs
)

Among them, Einter represents the interaction energy between the water molecules and CQDs, Etotal is the total energy of the water-CQDs system. E water is the energy of the water molecules. ECQDs is the energy of the CQDs.

CA and EDA were chosen to fabricate functional CQDs via the hydrothermal method at 150 °C (Fig. 1-a). The obtained CQDs powder demonstrates a good dispersibility and a narrow particle size distribution in Fig. 1-b and c. The average particle size is about 4.07 nm, less than 10 nm. It means that the size of the prepared CQDs powder meets the size requirements of quantum dots. Thus, the prepared CQDs powder demonstrates the fluorescence characteristics in UV light from Fig. 1-d. For the chemical structures, a wide peak at 26.2° in the XRD spectrum (Fig. 1-e), indicates that the prepared CQDs is composed of amorphous carbons. The CQDs structure was also analyzed by FTIR (Fig. 1-f and Fig. S1 in Supporting Information), the typical absorption band at 3380 cm−1 is associated with OH [59]. In addition, the new absorption bands at 3260 cm−1 and 2590 cm−1 were assigned for stretching vibrations of NH and CH, respectively. The absorption bands around 1700 and 1650 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CO, and the absorption bands around 1540 and 1400 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CC. Notably the CN and COC typical absorption band appears at 1170 cm−1 and 1080 cm−1. The above results show that the surface of the prepared CQDs contains functional hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups.

To further confirm the chemical structures of CQDs, XPS was employed to analyze the surface elemental composition. As shown in Fig. 2 , there are three characteristic peaks corresponding to C (284.85 eV), N (400.18 eV), and O (531.87 eV) in the full spectrum, and the contents of C, N, and O atoms in the CQDs surface were 72.88%, 8.64% and 18.48% respectively. For the detailed structures in Fig. 2(b-d), the C1s signals can be deconvoluted into several peaks at 284.81, 285.28, 287.92 and 288.68 eV, which are attributed to CC, CN, CO, and CO groups [60], respectively. The N1s spectrum shows a peak at 400.21 eV, attributed to NH. The O1s spectrum can be divided into two peaks at 531.84 and 533.66 eV, corresponding to the CO and CO, respectively. The results from XPS indicated that the surfaces of CQDs were functionalized by hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups, which are consistent with the results of FTIR.

The synthesis process of cross-linked qauternized polysulfone (CQPSf) is shown in Scheme 1. As shown in Fig. 3 , the chemical structures of pure polysulfone (PSf), chloromethylated polysulfone (CMPSf), and cross-linking qauternized polysulfone (CQPSF) were revealed by 1H NMR testing. Compared with PSf, CMPSf shows a new characteristic peak at 4.53 ppm, which is assigned to protons of H on chloromethyl-CH2Cl. This confirms to the presence of chloromethyl groups in CMPSf side chains. After quaternization modification, the characteristic peak of chloromethyl (-CH2Cl) located at 4.5 ppm (CMPSf, Fig. 3-b) almost disappeared in the CQPSf side chain (Fig. 3-c), and a new typical peak appeared at 3.05 ppm, which is assigned to H in quaternary ammonium group [61]. It is implied that the CQPSf was successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterizations of hybrid AEMs
ce:section.ce:para: The actual photos of fabricated membranes are shown in Fig. 4 (b). The pristine CMPSf AEM is transparent. After incorporating slightly yellow CQDs, the hybrid membrane becomes yellow and transparent. When taking them into a 365 nm wavelength UV lamp, the hybrid membrane presents uniform fluorescence and without unlike the new membrane (Fig. 4(c)). These macroscopic phenomena indicate that the obtained membranes are complete without defects, and the CQDs have a uniform distribution in the polymer skeleton. Also, these are proved by the SEM. In Fig. 4(d, e), the prepared AEMs are uniform and defect-free.

The FTIR spectra for the hybrid AEMs is presented in Fig. 4(f). The typical absorption peaks for PSf appeared at 1582 cm−1, and 1484 cm−1, 1232 cm−1, 1292 cm−1, and 1145 cm−1 are attributed to the CC skeletal vibrations of aromatic hydrocarbon, 1,4-aromatic ring substitution vibrations, antisymmetric vibrations of ether linkage, symmetric and asymmetric [62], respectively. A new signal peak at 1620 cm−1, which was caused by quaternary ammonium groups, indicates that the PSf backbone was successfully grafted with stretching vibration of CH. These results are well consistent with 1H NMR analysis results. These further prove the successful quaternization of chloromethylated PSf.

In addition, the pristine CQPSf and CQPSf-1% CQDs hybrid membranes were selected to analyze hydrophilic ion cluster domains in the membrane by the small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS). The size of the ion clusters is related to q values in SAXS. Fig. 4(g, h) shows the SAXS images of CQPSf and CQPSf-1% CQDs AEMs, respectively. The peaks of high q and high intensity in SAXS are not very obvious, so it is impossible to prove that the doping of quantum dots can improve the hydrophilicity of the films based on the characteristic Bragg spacing (d) [63]. However, as shown in the below water contact angel test (part 3.5), with the addition of CQDs, the water contact angle of hybrid films decreases. Namely, the addition of CQDs can improve the hydrophilicity of hybrid films. As a result, the ionic conductivity of the membrane is improved, which also occurs in the other composite films of CQDs [50,64–66]. Thus, the proposed transport mechanism is shown in Fig. 4(a). The incorporated CQDs interact weakly with cationic groups in the side chains through hydrogen bonds. Then, the CQDs may play a role in the bridge action for the
O H
-
transportation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal and mechanical stabilities of hybrid AEMs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5 (a), all CQPSf membranes exhibit a three-step degradation profile. The first degradation stage appeared between 30 and 170 °C, which is mainly attributed to the evaporation of solvent and water on or in membranes. The second thermal weight loss stage appeared between 170 and 400 °C, due to the thermolysis of the quaternary ammonium groups on the polymer side-chains. The third stage occurred over 400 °C, which was mainly attributed to the thermolysis of polymer skeletons. From Fig. 5(a), the thermogravimetric loss reduced slightly with the addition of CQDs. The addition of CQDs doesn't affect the practical application of hybrid mem-branes. The obtained hybrid membranes satisfy the practical operating environments for fuel cells, which are similar to that of most inorganic-doped composite membranes [12,55,67].

Fig. 5(b) shows the mechanical properties of CQPSf hybrid AEMs with different contents of CQDs, which were measured at 25 °C in a dry environment. The mechanical properties of CQPSf hybrid AEMs were higher than that of the pristine AEMs, with a maximum increase of about 21% for the CQPSf-2.5%-CQDs membrane. Meanwhile, Young's modulus of AEMs were increased from 1206 to 1372 MPa. However, with the increase of CQDs continuously, the Tensile strength and Young's modulus of AEMs began to drop significantly, even lower than that of the pristine one. This phenomenon is because of the agglomeration of CQDs, which is similar to the TGA analysis. In addition, as shown in Table S1 in Supporting Information, the elongation at break for all CQPSf was lower than that of QPSf due to the cross-linked network structure. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the doping of quantum dots improves the tensile strength of the membrane, but the elongation at break decreases with the amount of doping. It may be due to the rigid structure of the membrane. When the inorganic material fills into the polymer main chain and the flexible side chain, it could hinder the movement of the polymer chain, thereby decreasing the flexibility of the hybrid membrane. This phenomenon has been observed in many other composite membranes [68–71]. Anyway, the elongation at break for all hybrid AEMs is still at an acceptable level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water uptakes, swelling ratios, and ion exchange capacities of hybrid AEMs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 6 (a, b), the WU and SR were tested from 30 and 60 °C. It can be seen that the WU and SR of hybrid CQPSf AEMs are almost all higher than that of the pristine ones. Moreover, the WU and SR could be further enhanced after adding CQDs, and increased with the increase of CQDs contents. These phenomena are attributed to the introduction of hydrophilic CQDs. As we know, the hydrophilic CQDs dispersed in the membrane can expand the hydrophilic areas in AEMs. At the same time, the cross-linked network structures provide a unique space for CQDs movement, thereby promoting CQDS to diffuse and further expand the hydrophilic areas of AEMs. To prove these, the surface hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity of AEMs were further evaluated by measuring the water contact angle of the pristine and hybrid membranes in Fig. 6(c). It can be seen that the water contact angles of the hybrid AEMs are all lower than that of the pristine membranes. It is implied that the introduction of CQDs enables the membrane surfaces to attract more water molecules, thereby enhancing the WU and SR of AEMs. Anyway, the WU and SR of all cross-linked AEMs (CQPSf) are far below that of the uncross-linked membrane (QPSf) in Table S2 (Supporting Information). All hybrid AEMs can maintain an excellent dimensional stability at 60 °C.

The IEC of AEMs reflects the density of conductive groups per unit weight of membranes, which affects the WU, SR, and conductivity of the membranes. As summarized in Fig. 6(d) and Table S2, the IEC values increased with the increase of CQDs contents. The highest value was about 1.53 mmol g−1 for CQPSf-2.5%-CQDs, rising by 26.4%. However, with the rise in CQDs content, the IEC value had little change, even decreased, which is due to the aggregation of CQDs. Besides, experimental IEC values of different CQPSf AEMs ranged from 1.21 to 1.53 mmol g−1, which were all higher than that of the QPSf membrane (1.16 mmol g−1) and commercial Nafion115 (0.72 mmol g−1) [72]. Namely, the fabricated hybrid CQPSf AEMs own an excellent ion transfer efficiency and have application prospects in the field of fuel cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anion conductivity and alkaline stability of hybrid AEMs
ce:section.ce:para:
The ionic conductivity of hybrid AEMs was measured via the two-electrode method, and the conductive ion was hydroxyl ion (
O H
-
). The
O H
-
conductivity curves of the CQPSf pristine and hybrid membranes at different temperatures are shown in Fig. 7 (a). As shown in it, the hybrid AEMs with doping amounts of 1 wt.% and 2.5 wt.% are higher than the pristine ones, and the hybrid AEMs with 1 wt.% CQDs have the highest ionic conductivity, reaching 61.1 mS cm−1. This is because the free CQDs expand the hydrophilic area in polymer skeletons, thereby increasing the active space of
O H
-
in the membrane, and promoting ion transport. The high ion transport performance has certain application potential in fuel cells. However, when the doping amount of CQDs is 5 wt.%, a significant decrease in ionic conductivity appears and is even lower than that of the pristine one. As shown in above Fig. 6(a), when the water loading is increased, the high concentration of CQDs couldn’t be well dispersed in the membrane, resulting in a large-area agglomeration and hindering the
O H
-
transport channel. Moreover, the agglomerated CQDs and polysulfone network may complicate intra-membrane structures, increase the hydroxide ion transport path, and reduce the transport rate. Besides, the Arrhenius curves of different AEMs are given in Fig. S2 in Supporting Information. Using these curves, the apparent activation energies (E a) of AEMs could be calculated by the equation of
E a
= - b R
, where b is the straight line slope and R is the gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1). Then, the E a for different AEMs could be obtained, which are between 1.43–2.22 kJ mol−1.

The alkaline stabilities of hybrid AEMs were estimated via the evolution of ionic conductivity after soaking in a 1.0 M NaOH solution at 60 °C for different times. As can be seen from Fig. 7(b), there is little difference in the residual conductivity for the pristine and CQPSf-1%CQDs membranes before soaking 72 h, indicating that both the two types of membranes could maintain good stability for soaking for 72 h. However, after soaking 72 h, the residual conductivity of the CQPSf-1%CQDs membrane is always higher than that of pristine one. After soaking 168 h, the residual conductivity of the CQPSf-1%CQDs membrane still maintains at about 61.6%, showing an excellent alkali resistance stability. On the contrary, the pristine membrane was broken due to excessive degradation, and no data were obtained for soaking 168 h. This phenomenon is attributed to the incorporation of CQDs with hydrophilic surface and internal rigidity into the membrane matrix. Meanwhile, these CQDs were precisely dispersed into and near the polymer main chain and ion conducting group, which could limit the influx of hydroxyl ions into the polymer main chain and ion conducting group, thereby preventing their erosion by hydroxyl ions. Thus, the fabricated hybrid membrane has excellent alkali resistance stability.

Why can CQDs enhance the anion conductivity of AEMs? The water loading around the CQDs and in AEMs is a crucial factor. In order to further explore the mechanisms of CQDs in enhancing the water loading of AEM, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations were used to evaluate the influence of CQDs on the hydrophilic area in the membrane matrix. The calculation model is shown in Fig. 8 (a-d). It was found that water molecules were immobilized on the carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of CQDs (taking NO-CQDs as an example) through hydrogen bonding, indicating that the polarity of CQDs depends on the surface groups. The interaction energy and the number of intramolecular hydrogen bonds between GQDs, O-CQDs, and NO-CQDs and water molecules are shown in Fig. 8(e). It can be observed that the minimum interaction energy between GQDs and water molecules is only about −55 kJ mol−1. The interaction energy between O-CQDs and water molecules is about −98 kJ mol−1, and the number of hydrogen bonds is 9, indicating that O-CQDs have certain hydrophilicity. The NO-CQDs obtained the strongest interaction energy with about −107 kJ mol−1, and the number of hydrogen bonds was 11. It may be that the interaction energy between NO-CQDs and water molecules is enhanced, which improves the water capture ability. MSD simulations of water molecules and hydroxides in CQDs, O-CQDs and NO-CQDs systems were carried out. The self-diffusion coefficient (D) was calculated using the following equation:
D =
m 6
, where m denotes the slope of MSD curve. It is obvious from Fig. 8(f) that the slope of NO-GQDs is higher than that of O-GQDs and GQDs, which is consistent with the result of water molecule interaction energy. The hydrophilic area around CQDs expands and builds a well-defined microphase-separated structure. At the same time, hydroxide ions are continuously transported in the AEM through the formation and breaking of hydrogen bonds. Due to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the surface of CQDs and water molecules, a new transport channel is opened to transfer hydroxide ions.

As shown in Table 1 , the prepared hybrid AEMs in this work were compared with some other AEMs. It can be seen that the prepared hybrid membranes own excellent dimensional stabilities and ion transport performances. Nevertheless, the ionic conductivities of the prepared AEMs are lower than that doped with quaternized graphite oxide [73], which may be due to the lack of ion exchange groups on CQDs surfaces in this work. Moreover, the preliminary fuel cell properties for the application of CQPSf hybrid AEMs were carried out in a methanol single-cell. It can be seen from Fig. 9 , the open circuit voltage of the fuel cell is 0.56 V. At 60 °C, the limiting current density is 22.29 mA cm−2, with a maximum power density of 4.52 mW cm−2. Obviously, the power density is lower than that of published literatures. Anyway, these preliminary results give us a lot of hopes for further experiments and optimizations for the hybrid AEMs.

In summary, CQDs hybrid and cross-linking modification via side-chain designing were combined to regulate and control the performances of AEMs successfully. Then a type of organic–inorganic hybrid CQPSf-CQDs AEMs has abstained. After hybrid and cross-linking, the mechanical properties of CQPSf hybrid AEMs were higher than that of the pristine AEMs. Meanwhile, Young's modulus of AEMs was increased from 1206 to 1372 MPa. The existence of CQDs can expand the hydrophilic area in the membrane, which is beneficial to the transport of
O H
-
. The incorporated CQDs interact weakly with cationic groups in the side chains through hydrogen bonds. The CQDs may play a role in the bridge action for the
O H
-
transportation. The simulation results further demonstrated that the addition of CQDs was beneficial to constructing hydrophilic and hydrophobic domains, thereby increasing
O H
-
conductivity through the membrane. Also, the prepared hybrid membranes own excellent dimensional stabilities and ion transport performances. For the preliminary direct methanol fuel cell properties, despite the power density being lower than that of published literatures, these preliminary results give us a lot of hopes for further experiments and optimizations for the hybrid AEMs.

Yuan Jin: Conceptualization, Data curation, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Xiu Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Formal analysis. Tianci Feng: Formal analysis. Meisheng Li: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Huifang Xiao: Project administration. Shouyong Zhou: Funding acquisition, Project administration. Yijiang Zhao: Supervision, Project administration. Jing Zhong: Project administration. Dawei Yang: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21878118), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20211368), Jiangsu Province Qing Lan Project for the Young Academic Leaders (Meisheng Li, 2021), Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (21KJB530002), and Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Regional Modern Agriculture & Environmental Protection (HSXT3063).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2022.08.003.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced conductivity of anion-exchange membrane by incorporation of quaternized cellulose nanocrystal

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Cheng, J. Wang, Y. Liao, C. Li, Z. Wei, Enhanced conductivity of anion-exchange membrane by incorporation of quaternized cellulose nanocrystal, ACS applied materials & interfaces 10(28) (2018) 23774-23782.

D. Zhang, S. Xu, R. Wan, Y. Yang, R. He, Functionalized graphene oxide cross-linked poly (2, 6-dimethyl-1, 4-phenylene oxide)-based anion exchange membranes with superior ionic conductivity, Journal of Power Sources 517 (2022) 230720.

G. Liu, W.-C. Tsen, S.-C. Jang, F. Hu, F. Zhong, B. Zhang, J. Wang, H. Liu, G. Wang, S. Wen, Composite membranes from quaternized chitosan reinforced with surface-functionalized PVDF electrospun nanofibers for alkaline direct methanol fuel cells, Journal of Membrane Science 611 (2020) 118242.

K. Hooshyari, H. Rezania, V. Vatanpour, M. Rastgoo‐Deylami, H.R. Rajabi, New blend nanocomposite membranes based on PBI/sulfonated poly (ether keto imide sulfone) and functionalized quantum dot with improved fuel cell performance at high temperatures, International Journal of Energy Research 45(15) (2021) 21274-21292.

J. Ren, Y. Dong, J. Dai, H. Hu, Y. Zhu, X. Teng, A novel chloromethylated/quaternized poly (sulfone)/poly (vinylidene fluoride) anion exchange membrane with ultra-low vanadium permeability for all vanadium redox flow battery, Journal of Membrane Science 544 (2017) 186-194.

D. Zhang, N. Ye, S. Chen, R. Wan, Y. Yang, R. He, Enhancing properties of poly (2, 6-dimethyl-1, 4-phenylene oxide)-based anion exchange membranes with 5-mercaptotetrazole modified graphene oxides, Renewable Energy 160 (2020) 250-260.
Y. Yang, J. Wang, J. Zheng, S. Li, S. Zhang, A stable anion exchange membrane based on imidazolium salt for alkaline fuel cell, Journal of membrane science 467 (2014) 48-55.

P. Msomi, P. Nonjola, P. Ndungu, J. Ramontja, Poly (2, 6-dimethyl-1, 4-phenylene)/polysulfone anion exchange membrane blended with TiO2 with improved water uptake for alkaline fuel cell application, International Journal of Hydrogen Energy 45(53) (2020) 29465-29476.
